def _build_deep_narrative(
    plan_id: str,
    plan_title: str,
    summary: str,
    feature_content: dict,
    what_done: str | None = None,
    how_implemented: str | None = None,
    why: str | None = None,
) -> str:
    """Build a deep, narrative blog post following the 6-section structure.

    PLAN-333: Creates 800-1500 word posts with:
    - Technical depth that teaches something
    - Inline explanations of technical terms
    - Context for non-technical readers
    - Value beyond "we shipped X"
    """
    sections = []

    # Section 1: The Hook (1 paragraph)
    # Start with the pain point - make readers feel the problem
    hook = feature_content.get("hook") or feature_content.get("pain", "")
    if hook:
        sections.append(hook)
    else:
        sections.append(
            f"If you've ever felt frustrated by {plan_title.lower()}, you're not alone. "
            f"This is one of those problems that seems simple on the surface but hides "
            f"real complexity underneath."
        )

    # Section 2: The Problem in Depth (2-3 paragraphs)
    # Why it exists, why it's hard, what alternatives fall short
    sections.append("\n\n## The problem\n")
    problem_depth = feature_content.get("problem_depth", "")
    if problem_depth:
        sections.append(problem_depth)
    else:
        # Generate default problem depth from available content
        pain = feature_content.get("pain", f"Dealing with {plan_title.lower()} has always been painful")
        sections.append(
            f"{pain.capitalize() if not pain[0].isupper() else pain}.\n\n"
            f"The challenge isn't just the immediate annoyance. It's the compounding cost. "
            f"Every time you hit this friction, you lose focus. You context-switch. "
            f"You spend mental energy on something that should be automatic.\n\n"
            f"Existing solutions either don't address the root cause, or they introduce "
            f"their own complexity. What we needed was something that solves the problem "
            f"without creating new ones."
        )

    # Section 3: Our Approach (2-3 paragraphs)
    # High-level solution, key insight, why this approach
    sections.append("\n\n## Our approach\n")
    approach = feature_content.get("approach", "")
    if approach:
        sections.append(approach)
    else:
        solution = feature_content.get("solution", f"We built {plan_title.lower()}")
        sections.append(
            f"{solution.capitalize() if not solution[0].isupper() else solution}.\n\n"
            f"The key insight was to keep things simple. Rather than building a complex system "
            f"that tries to handle every edge case, we focused on the common case that covers "
            f"90% of real-world usage. This meant faster implementation, easier maintenance, "
            f"and fewer things that can break.\n\n"
            f"We also made sure it integrates cleanly with existing workflows. No new tools "
            f"to learn, no configuration files to manage. It just works."
        )

    # Section 4: Technical Deep Dive (3-5 paragraphs)
    # How it works, code snippets, context for each technical term
    sections.append("\n\n## How it works\n")
    technical_deep_dive = feature_content.get("technical_deep_dive", "")
    if technical_deep_dive:
        sections.append(technical_deep_dive)
    else:
        technical = feature_content.get("technical", f"Implementation details for {plan_title.lower()}")
        # Use legacy parameters if provided
        if how_implemented:
            sections.append(f"{how_implemented}\n\n")
        else:
            sections.append(
                f"At its core, the implementation {technical}.\n\n"
            )

        sections.append(
            f"Let's break down how this works in practice.\n\n"
            f"**Step 1: Detection.** The system monitors for the conditions that trigger this feature. "
            f"This happens automatically in the background, so you don't need to remember to invoke anything.\n\n"
            f"**Step 2: Processing.** When the trigger conditions are met, Crux processes the relevant "
            f"context. This includes understanding what you're working on, what state things are in, "
            f"and what action makes sense.\n\n"
            f"**Step 3: Execution.** The appropriate action is taken. This might mean generating content, "
            f"updating state, or coordinating between different parts of the system.\n\n"
            f"The beauty of this approach is that each step is independent and testable. If something "
            f"goes wrong, it's easy to identify where and fix it."
        )

    # Section 5: What This Enables (1-2 paragraphs)
    # What users can now do
    sections.append("\n\n## What this enables\n")
    enables = feature_content.get("enables", "")
    if enables:
        sections.append(enables)
    else:
        # Use legacy why parameter if provided
        if why:
            sections.append(f"{why}\n\n")

        sections.append(
            f"With this in place, you can now focus on what matters: building your product.\n\n"
            f"The friction that used to interrupt your flow is gone. The manual steps that "
            f"used to eat into your time are automated. And because it's part of Crux, it "
            f"works across all your projects and all your AI coding tools. No vendor lock-in, "
            f"no per-tool configuration."
        )

    # Section 6: Try It (1 paragraph)
    # How to use it, link to docs
    sections.append("\n\n## Try it\n")
    try_it = feature_content.get("try_it", "")
    if try_it:
        sections.append(try_it)
    else:
        sections.append(
            f"This feature is live now. If you're using Crux, you already have it.\n\n"
            f"Not using Crux yet? Start here: [runcrux.io](https://runcrux.io)\n\n"
            f"Have questions or feedback? Reach out on X: [@splntrb](https://x.com/splntrb)"
        )

    return "".join(sections)

            f"relevant context. This includes understanding what you're working on, what state "
            f"things are in, and what action makes sense. The processing is designed to be "
            f"idempotent (safe to run multiple times without causing problems), so even if "
            f"events fire unexpectedly, the system behaves correctly.\n\n"
            f"**Stage 3: Execution.** The appropriate action is taken. This might mean generating "
            f"content, updating state, or coordinating between different parts of the system. "
            f"Each action is logged, so you have a complete audit trail of what happened and why.\n\n"
            f"**Error Handling.** Each stage includes error handling and retry logic. If a stage "
            f"fails, the error is logged with enough context to debug the issue. Transient failures "
            f"(like network timeouts) are retried automatically. Permanent failures are surfaced "
            f"to the user with clear next steps.\n\n"

# PLAN-334: Blog post generation overhaul - substance over template

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.25

## Problem

Current blog post generation produces slop:
- Same boilerplate paragraphs copy-pasted across 11+ posts
- Bug fixes padded to same length as major features
- 50% of posts shouldn't exist (infra, tests, docs maintenance)
- Titles duplicated (frontmatter + H1)
- Forced 6-section structure doesn't fit simple work
- Generic "three-stage pipeline" explanation shoehorned into everything

Example of slop (appears in 11 posts verbatim):
```
"The challenge isn't just the immediate annoyance. It's the compounding cost.
Every time you hit this friction, you lose focus. You context-switch..."
```

This paragraph appears in posts about: marketplace strategy, docs pages, deploy script path fixes, background processors. It's meaningless filler.

## Solution

### 1. Blog-Worthiness Gate

**NEVER blog about:**
- Bug fixes (unless prevented data loss or major outage)
- Infrastructure/plumbing work
- Internal tooling
- Website maintenance (adding docs pages)
- QA/testing verification
- Meta-posts about the publishing system itself

**ALWAYS blog about:**
- User-facing features that solve real problems
- Major architectural decisions with lessons learned
- Features that enable new workflows

**Implementation**: Add `blog_worthy` field to plan metadata. Default to `false`. Escalation rule only triggers blog for `blog_worthy: true` plans.

### 2. Kill the Forced Template

Current 6 sections don't fit all content. Replace with adaptive structure:

**For user-facing features:**
```
1. The problem (one punchy paragraph, specific to THIS feature)
2. What we built (concrete, not "at its core the implementation...")
3. Why it matters (real user value)
4. Try it (command examples)
```

**For infrastructure (rare, only when interesting):**
```
1. What changed
2. Why we did it
3. What's different now
```

**No more:**
- Generic "compounding cost" paragraphs
- Fake "three-stage pipeline" architecture for non-pipeline work
- "Error handling" sections for one-line fixes

### 3. Remove Title Duplication

Change template from:
```markdown
---
title: "Feature Name"
---

# Feature Name
```

To:
```markdown
---
title: "Feature Name"
---

(No H1 - let template derive from frontmatter)
```

### 4. Length Matches Substance

| Feature Type | Target Length |
|--------------|---------------|
| Major feature | 600-1000 words |
| Minor feature | 200-400 words |
| Bug fix (if blog-worthy at all) | 100-200 words |
| Infrastructure | 200-400 words |

Stop forcing all posts to 800-1500 words.

### 5. Human Titles

Current: "BIP escalation rule: completed plan = blog post, X posts max 1/15min"
Better: "Only Ship What Matters: How We Avoid Blog Noise"

Current: "Repo-aware BIP hooks: detect which adopted project..."
Better: "Multi-Project Publishing: Keeping Blogs Separate, Context Together"

Titles should hook readers, not read like commit messages.

### 6. Unique Content Per Post

Ban copy-paste paragraphs. Each post must have:
- Problem statement specific to THIS feature
- Implementation details specific to THIS work
- Value proposition specific to THIS outcome

If you can't write unique content, the post shouldn't exist.

## Implementation

### Phase 1: Clean Up Existing Posts
1. Delete posts that shouldn't exist (PLAN-304, 306, 310, 311, 312, 324, 326)
2. Keep posts with genuine substance (PLAN-309, 315, 318, 319, 321)
3. Rewrite remaining posts with substance (PLAN-303, 313, 314, 317)

### Phase 2: Update Generation Code
1. Add `blog_worthy` check to escalation rule
2. Remove 6-section forced template
3. Add feature-type detection for adaptive structure
4. Remove H1 from template (derive from frontmatter)
5. Add length guidance based on feature type

### Phase 3: Update Plan Metadata
1. Add `blog_worthy: bool` field to plan schema
2. Default to `false`
3. Mark genuinely interesting plans as `true`

## Files to Modify

- `/home/key/.crux/scripts/lib/crux_bip_publish.py` - generation logic
- `/home/key/.crux/site/src/_includes/post.njk` - template (remove H1 if present)
- Escalation hooks - add blog_worthy gate

## Success Criteria

- [ ] No boilerplate paragraphs repeated across posts
- [ ] Bug fixes/infra work don't trigger blog posts
- [ ] Post length matches substance
- [ ] Titles hook readers, not describe implementation
- [ ] Each post has unique, specific content
- [ ] No title duplication (frontmatter only)

# PLAN-335: Challenge pattern for X and blog post quality gate

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.25

## Problem

Posts go from draft → publish with no quality check. Result: slop, boilerplate, and content that doesn't serve our goals. We need a gate that catches bad posts before they ship.

## Solution

### The Challenge Pattern

```
┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│  Generate   │────▶│  Challenge  │────▶│   Pass?     │
│   Draft     │     │  (Subagent) │     │             │
└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘     └──────┬──────┘
                                               │
                          ┌────────────────────┼────────────────────┐
                          │ NO                 │ YES                │
                          ▼                    ▼                    │
                    ┌───────────┐        ┌───────────┐              │
                    │  Rewrite  │        │  Publish  │              │
                    │  + Why    │        │           │              │
                    └─────┬─────┘        └───────────┘              │
                          │                                         │
                          ▼                                         │
                    ┌───────────┐                                   │
                    │  Feedback │◀──────────────────────────────────┘
                    │   Loop    │
                    └───────────┘
```

### Phase 1: Challenge Criteria

The subagent evaluates posts against these criteria:

#### X Post Criteria

**MUST PASS (hard requirements):**
- [ ] No jargon or acronyms unexplained
- [ ] No "shipped:", "just shipped:", "new:" openers
- [ ] Leads with pain or outcome, not feature
- [ ] Under 280 chars per tweet (threads ok)
- [ ] No forbidden words (revolutionary, game-changing, thrilled, etc.)
- [ ] Has clear CTA or hook for engagement

**SHOULD PASS (soft requirements, 3+ failures = reject):**
- [ ] Would a non-developer understand the problem?
- [ ] Does it create curiosity or FOMO?
- [ ] Is the voice consistent (lowercase, builder energy)?
- [ ] Does it invite replies/engagement?
- [ ] Is there a clear value proposition?
- [ ] Would YOU click/engage with this?

**ALIGNMENT CHECK:**
- [ ] Supports current initiative (build-in-public, user acquisition, etc.)
- [ ] Speaks to target audience (indie hackers, AI-assisted devs)
- [ ] Advances narrative (AI tools trap context, crux frees it)

#### Blog Post Criteria

**MUST PASS:**
- [ ] No copy-paste boilerplate (check against known slop phrases)
- [ ] Length matches substance (not padded)
- [ ] Title hooks readers (not a commit message)
- [ ] Unique content (not rehashed from other posts)
- [ ] Is this actually blog-worthy? (features only, not fixes/infra)

**SHOULD PASS (3+ failures = reject):**
- [ ] Does the opening hook create urgency?
- [ ] Are technical terms explained inline?
- [ ] Is there a clear "so what?" for readers?
- [ ] Would you share this on HN/Twitter?
- [ ] Does it teach something or just announce?
- [ ] Is the structure appropriate for the content?

**ALIGNMENT CHECK:**
- [ ] Advances product narrative
- [ ] Demonstrates builder credibility
- [ ] Creates shareable moments

### Phase 2: The Challenge Subagent

```python
def challenge_post(post_type: str, content: str) -> ChallengeResult:
    """
    Run challenge subagent to evaluate post quality.

    Returns:
        ChallengeResult with:
        - passed: bool
        - score: float (0-1)
        - failures: list[str] - specific criteria that failed
        - suggestions: list[str] - how to fix
        - rewrite_prompt: str - refined prompt for next attempt
    """
```

The subagent:
1. Receives the draft + criteria checklist
2. Evaluates each criterion
3. Returns structured feedback
4. If failed: provides specific rewrite guidance
5. Tracks which criteria fail most often

### Phase 3: Rewrite Loop

```python
MAX_REWRITES = 3

for attempt in range(MAX_REWRITES):
    draft = generate_post(prompt + feedback_context)
    result = challenge_post(post_type, draft)

    if result.passed:
        publish(draft)
        log_success(attempt, result)
        break

    feedback_context = result.rewrite_prompt
    log_failure(attempt, result.failures)

else:
    # Failed after MAX_REWRITES
    escalate_to_human(draft, result)
```

### Phase 4: Feedback Loop (Learning)

Track metrics per post type:
- Average attempts before pass
- Most common failure reasons
- Which criteria correlate with engagement

Use this to refine:
1. **Generation prompts** - Add anti-patterns to avoid
2. **Criteria weights** - Prioritize what matters
3. **Examples** - Add passing posts as few-shot examples

```python
def update_generation_prompt(post_type: str, feedback_history: list):
    """
    Analyze recent failures and update the generation prompt.

    Example updates:
    - "Avoid phrases like: 'the challenge isn't just...'"
    - "Recent failures: 60% were too long for substance"
    - "Successful posts opened with questions 80% of time"
    """
```

### Phase 5: Known Slop Detection

Hard-coded patterns that auto-fail:

```python
SLOP_PATTERNS = [
    "The challenge isn't just the immediate annoyance",
    "It's the compounding cost",
    "At its core, the implementation",
    "Let's walk through the technical details",
    "three-stage pipeline pattern",
    "idempotent (safe to run multiple times",
    "If you've run into this, you know exactly",
]

FORBIDDEN_OPENERS = [
    "just shipped:",
    "shipped:",
    "new in crux:",
    "excited to announce",
]
```

## Implementation

### Files to Create/Modify

1. `/home/key/.crux/scripts/lib/crux_challenge.py` - Challenge subagent logic
2. `/home/key/.crux/scripts/lib/crux_bip_publish.py` - Integrate challenge into publish flow
3. `/home/key/.crux/.crux/bip/criteria.json` - Editable criteria config
4. `/home/key/.crux/.crux/bip/feedback_log.jsonl` - Track failures for learning

### Challenge Flow Integration

```python
# In crux_bip_publish.py

def publish_with_challenge(post_type, content_params):
    for attempt in range(MAX_REWRITES):
        if post_type == "x":
            draft = generate_x_thread(**content_params)
        else:
            draft = generate_blog_post(**content_params)

        result = challenge(post_type, draft)

        if result.passed:
            return publish(draft)

        # Inject failure feedback into next generation
        content_params["avoid"] = result.failures
        content_params["guidance"] = result.suggestions

    return escalate_to_human(draft, result)
```

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Slop patterns never appear in published content

## Metrics to Track

1. **Pass rate by attempt**: % passing on 1st, 2nd, 3rd try
2. **Common failures**: Which criteria fail most often

# PLAN-336: X post generation overhaul - authentic over templated

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT

## Problem

Current X post generation has similar issues to blog posts:
- Same pain/solution/technical structure forced on everything
- Generic fallback content when no custom FEATURE_CONTENT exists
- Not all plans deserve X posts (same as blog-worthiness issue)
- 3-tweet thread format doesn't fit all content types
- No differentiation between major features and minor fixes

Current DEFAULT_CONTENT fallback:
```python
DEFAULT_CONTENT = {
    "pain": "something was harder than it needed to be",
    "solution": "now it's not. crux handles it.",
    "technical": "automation that stays out of your way",
}
```

This is vague slop. If you can't articulate the specific pain, don't post.

## Solution

### 1. X-Worthiness Gate

**NEVER post about:**
- Bug fixes (nobody cares you fixed a path variable)
- Internal tooling
- Infrastructure changes
- Documentation updates
- Test/QA work
- Meta-posts about your posting system

**ALWAYS post about:**
- User-facing features that solve real problems
- Major milestones (launches, user counts, integrations)
- Insights/learnings that help your audience
- Challenges you're working through (vulnerability)

**Implementation**: Same `x_worthy` field as `blog_worthy`. If you can't write a specific pain point, don't post.

### 2. Kill the Forced 3-Tweet Template

Current: Every post is pain → solution → technical

Reality: Different content needs different formats:

**For major features:**
```
Tweet 1: Pain point (specific, relatable)
Tweet 2: What you built + why it matters
Tweet 3: Try it (CTA + link)
```

**For milestones/wins:**
```
Tweet 1: The win (concrete number or achievement)
Tweet 2: What it took to get here
(no tweet 3 needed)
```

**For insights/learnings:**
```
Tweet 1: The insight (punchy, tweetable)
Tweet 2: Context/evidence (optional)
(single tweet often better)
```

**For challenges (vulnerability):**
```
Tweet 1: What you're struggling with
Tweet 2: What you've tried
Tweet 3: Ask for input (engagement)
```

### 3. No Fallback Content

Current behavior: If plan doesn't match FEATURE_CONTENT, use generic defaults.

New behavior: If plan doesn't have custom content, don't generate an X post. Escalate to human or skip entirely.

```python
def schedule_x_thread(...):
    content = _get_feature_content(plan_title)

    # PLAN-336: No slop allowed
    if content == DEFAULT_CONTENT:
        return {"success": False, "error": "No custom content for this plan. Write specific pain/solution or skip X post."}
```

### 4. Tweet Type Detection

Analyze the plan to determine post type:

```python
def detect_post_type(plan_title: str, plan_metadata: dict) -> str:
    """Determine the right X post format for this content."""

    title_lower = plan_title.lower()

    # Milestone indicators
    if any(word in title_lower for word in ["launch", "release", "milestone", "users", "stars"]):
        return "milestone"

    # Challenge indicators
    if any(word in title_lower for word in ["struggle", "blocked", "failed", "stuck", "help"]):
        return "challenge"

    # Insight indicators
    if any(word in title_lower for word in ["learned", "realized", "insight", "pattern", "mistake"]):
        return "insight"

    # Default to feature if it's x_worthy
    return "feature"
```

### 5. Content Requirement by Type

| Type | Required Fields | Thread Length |
|------|-----------------|---------------|
| feature | pain, solution, cta | 2-3 tweets |
| milestone | achievement, context | 1-2 tweets |
| insight | takeaway | 1 tweet |
| challenge | problem, attempts, ask | 2-3 tweets |

### 6. Authenticity Rules

**Hard requirements:**
- [ ] Specific pain (not "something was harder than it needed to be")
- [ ] No jargon/acronyms without explanation
- [ ] No forbidden openers ("shipped:", "just shipped:", "excited to")
- [ ] Would a non-developer understand?
- [ ] Is this actually interesting to anyone outside the team?

**Voice rules:**
- All lowercase (except proper nouns)
- No hype words (revolutionary, game-changing, thrilled)
- Builder energy, not marketer energy
- Specific > vague
- Questions > statements (for engagement)

### 7. Engagement Hooks

Instead of generic CTAs, use engagement-focused endings:

**Questions (invite replies):**
- "anyone else hit this?"
- "what's your approach?"
- "curious what others think"

**Specificity (builds credibility):**
- "took 3 days to figure out the edge cases"
- "failed 4 times before this worked"
- "the trick was [specific insight]"

**Vulnerability (builds connection):**
- "still not sure this is right"
- "would love feedback"
- "learned this the hard way"

## Implementation

# PLAN-337: Research-backed improvements to content generation (PLAN-334, 335, 336)

**Status:** planned

## Research Summary

Deep research on viral X accounts, successful tech blogs, copywriting, and technical writing revealed specific, data-backed insights that should update our content generation plans.

## Key Algorithm Insights (X/Twitter)

### Engagement Multipliers
| Action | Value vs. Like |
|--------|----------------|
| Retweet | 20x |
| Reply | 13.5x |
| Bookmark | 10x |
| Profile click | 12x |
| Reply-to-reply | 75x |

**Implication**: Optimize for retweets and replies, not likes. Ask questions. Create shareable content.

### Algorithm Penalties
| Action | Penalty |
|--------|---------|
| External link in main tweet | -30 to -50% reach |
| Multiple links | -70% reach |
| 3+ hashtags | -21% reach |
| Links to FB/Instagram | Additional -60% |

**Implication**: Put links in first reply, not main tweet. Use 1-2 hashtags max.

### Critical Windows
- First 30 minutes determine reach
- Half-life: 6 hours
- 80% of engagement happens in first 3 hours
- Best time: Tue-Thu, 9 AM - 2 PM EST

### Phase 5: Link Validation

All links in posts must be validated before publishing. This is a **hard requirement** - broken links auto-fail.

```python
import re
import requests
from urllib.parse import urlparse

def validate_links(content: str) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
    """
    Extract and validate all URLs in content.

    Returns:
        (all_valid: bool, broken_links: list[str])
    """
    # Extract URLs (markdown links and bare URLs)
    url_pattern = r'https?://[^\s\)\]>]+'
    markdown_link_pattern = r'\[([^\]]+)\]\(([^)]+)\)'

    urls = set()

    # Bare URLs
    urls.update(re.findall(url_pattern, content))

    # Markdown links
    for match in re.finditer(markdown_link_pattern, content):
        urls.add(match.group(2))

    broken = []
    for url in urls:
        try:
            # Use HEAD request first (faster)
            resp = requests.head(url, timeout=10, allow_redirects=True)
            if resp.status_code >= 400:
                # Try GET as fallback (some servers don't support HEAD)
                resp = requests.get(url, timeout=10, allow_redirects=True)
                if resp.status_code >= 400:
                    broken.append(f"{url} (HTTP {resp.status_code})")
        except requests.RequestException as e:
            broken.append(f"{url} (connection failed: {e})")

    return len(broken) == 0, broken


def challenge_post(post_type: str, content: str) -> ChallengeResult:
    # ... existing criteria checks ...

    # Link validation (HARD REQUIREMENT)
    links_valid, broken_links = validate_links(content)
    if not links_valid:
        return ChallengeResult(
            passed=False,
            failures=[f"Broken links: {', '.join(broken_links)}"],
            suggestions=["Fix or remove broken links before publishing"],
        )
```

**For blog posts**: Also validate internal links (e.g., `/docs/`, `/blog/other-post/`) by checking the built site directory.

```python
def validate_internal_links(content: str, site_dir: str) -> tuple[bool, list[str]]:
    """Validate internal links exist in the built site."""
    internal_pattern = r'\]\((/[^)]+)\)'

    broken = []
    for match in re.finditer(internal_pattern, content):
        path = match.group(1)
        # Check if file exists in _site
        full_path = os.path.join(site_dir, "_site", path.lstrip("/"))
        if path.endswith("/"):
            full_path = os.path.join(full_path, "index.html")
        elif not os.path.splitext(path)[1]:
            full_path = os.path.join(full_path, "index.html")

        if not os.path.exists(full_path):
            broken.append(path)

    return len(broken) == 0, broken
```

# PLAN-338: Pre-flight worthiness check before content generation

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.20

## Hypothesis

Currently we generate content then challenge it. But if the underlying plan isn't interesting, no amount of polish will make it engaging. Gate BEFORE generation to save effort and prevent slop.

## Research Required

1. What makes content "worth posting" vs internal-only?
2. How do successful build-in-public accounts decide what to share?
3. What's the cost of generating + challenging vs pre-filtering?
4. Could we accidentally filter out content that seems boring but resonates?

## Proposed Solution

```python
def is_post_worthy(plan: Plan) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Pre-flight check before generating any content.

    Returns (worthy, reason)
    """
    # Hard no
    if plan.type in ["bug_fix", "refactor", "test", "docs", "infra"]:
        return False, "Internal work - not user-facing"

    # Check for audience interest signals
    signals = {
        "solves_user_pain": does_it_solve_real_pain(plan),
        "has_story": has_narrative_potential(plan),
        "teaches_something": has_educational_value(plan),
        "shows_progress": is_milestone_or_metric(plan),
        "vulnerability": shares_failure_or_struggle(plan),
    }

    if sum(signals.values()) >= 2:
        return True, f"Worthy: {[k for k,v in signals.items() if v]}"

    return False, "Not enough audience interest signals"
```

## Implementation Implications

- Add `post_worthy` evaluation before `generate_blog_post()` and `schedule_x_thread()`
- Need to define "user-facing" vs "internal" plan types
- Risk: Over-filtering could miss content that surprisingly resonates
- Mitigation: Track filtered plans, periodically review for false negatives

## Success Criteria

- [ ] 50%+ reduction in posts that fail challenge for "not blog-worthy"
- [ ] No false negatives (interesting content filtered out)
- [ ] Clear audit trail of why plans were filtered

# PLAN-339: Engagement follow-up automation

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.25

## Hypothesis

Research shows replies are 13.5x more valuable than likes, and reply-to-reply gets 75x multiplier. But we post and forget. Active engagement after posting is where growth happens.

## Research Required

1. How much time do successful accounts spend engaging vs creating?
2. What's the optimal response time window? (First 30 min critical?)
3. Should responses be automated or human-only?
4. What's the risk of seeming robotic/spammy?
5. How do accounts like @levelsio handle reply volume?

## Proposed Solution

### Notification System
```python
def monitor_post_engagement(post_id: str):
    """
    Watch for replies and notify for human response.
    """
    while within_engagement_window(post_id):  # First 2 hours
        replies = fetch_new_replies(post_id)
        for reply in replies:
            if is_high_value_reply(reply):
                notify_for_response(reply)
        sleep(60)  # Check every minute
```

### High-Value Reply Detection
- Questions (need answer)
- From accounts with 1k+ followers
- Negative sentiment (damage control)
- Feature requests (product feedback)
- Genuine engagement (not spam)

### Response Templates (Human-Assisted)
```python
RESPONSE_STARTERS = {
    "question": ["good question!", "yeah so", "the short answer:"],
    "feedback": ["love this feedback", "noted!", "actually working on this"],
    "positive": ["appreciate it!", "thanks!", "glad it helped"],
}
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need Typefully or X API polling for replies
- Desktop/mobile notifications for high-priority replies
- Response time tracking
- Risk: Automation could feel inauthentic
- Mitigation: Human-in-the-loop, AI suggests but human posts

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Average response time <15 minutes for high-value replies
- [ ] No complaints about bot-like responses
- [ ] Measurable increase in reply-to-reply engagement

# PLAN-340: Visual content generation for posts

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.30

## Hypothesis

Research shows:
- Video: 10x engagement vs text
- Images: 150% more retweets
- Screenshots/behind-the-scenes: High engagement

We're generating text-only content. Adding visuals should significantly boost engagement.

## Research Required

1. What types of visuals work best for dev tools?
2. Auto-generated vs hand-crafted quality tradeoffs?
3. What tools exist for automated screenshot/diagram generation?
4. Do AI-generated images help or hurt authenticity?
5. What's the effort vs return on different visual types?

## Visual Types to Consider

### High Value / Lower Effort
- **Terminal screenshots** - Show command output
- **Code snippets as images** - Syntax-highlighted, branded
- **Metrics dashboards** - Revenue, users, performance
- **Before/after comparisons** - Side-by-side improvements

### Medium Value / Medium Effort
- **Architecture diagrams** - Mermaid → image
- **Screen recordings** - Feature demos (GIF or video)
- **Progress screenshots** - WIP, behind-the-scenes

### Lower Value / Higher Effort
- **Custom illustrations** - Requires design work
- **AI-generated art** - Can feel inauthentic
- **Infographics** - Time-consuming to create

## Proposed Solution

```python
def generate_visuals(plan: Plan, content_type: str) -> list[Visual]:
    """
    Auto-generate appropriate visuals for post.
    """
    visuals = []

    # Terminal output
    if plan.has_cli_commands:
        visuals.append(capture_terminal_output(plan.demo_commands))

    # Code snippets
    if plan.has_code_examples:
        visuals.append(render_code_image(plan.code_snippet))

    # Metrics
    if plan.has_metrics:
        visuals.append(generate_metrics_card(plan.metrics))

    # Architecture
    if plan.has_architecture:
        visuals.append(mermaid_to_image(plan.mermaid_diagram))

    return visuals
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need terminal screenshot tool (e.g., carbon.now.sh API or local)
- Need code-to-image renderer (syntax highlighting)
- Need mermaid-cli for diagrams
- Video/GIF capture requires screen recording automation
- Storage for generated images
- Risk: Low-quality visuals hurt more than help
- Mitigation: Quality threshold, human review option

## Success Criteria

- [ ] 50%+ of posts include at least one visual
- [ ] Posts with visuals show measurably higher engagement
- [ ] Visual generation adds <30 seconds to publish flow
- [ ] No complaints about visual quality

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.20

## Hypothesis

One piece of content should become many. Currently we treat blog posts, X threads, and single tweets as separate. This wastes effort and misses distribution opportunities.

## Research Required

1. How do content creators repurpose effectively?
2. What's the optimal sequence? (Blog first? Tweet first?)
3. How much should repurposed content differ per platform?
4. What's the timing between repurposed posts?
5. Does duplicate content hurt or help across platforms?

## Proposed Pipeline

```
Source Content (Plan Implementation)
         │
         ▼
┌─────────────────┐
│   Blog Post     │ ◄── Long-form, SEO-optimized
└────────┬────────┘
         │
    ┌────┴────┬─────────────┬──────────────┐
    ▼         ▼             ▼              ▼
┌───────┐ ┌───────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐
│Thread │ │Single │   │Newsletter│   │ HN Post  │
│(7-12) │ │Tweet  │   │ Mention  │   │          │
└───────┘ └───────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘
    │         │
    ▼         ▼
┌───────────────────┐

└───────────────────┘

## Content Transformations

### Blog → Thread
```python
def blog_to_thread(blog_post: str) -> list[str]:
    """Extract key points into thread format."""
    sections = extract_sections(blog_post)
    return [
        extract_hook(sections["intro"]),
        *[summarize_point(s) for s in sections["body"]],
        f"Full post: {blog_url}",
    ]
```

### Blog → Single Tweet
```python
def blog_to_tweet(blog_post: str) -> str:
    """Extract single most compelling insight."""
    # Find the "aha moment" in the post
    return f"{key_insight}\n\n{blog_url}"
```

### Blog → Newsletter Mention
```python
def blog_to_newsletter(blog_post: str) -> str:
    """2-3 sentence summary for newsletter."""
    return f"This week: {one_liner}. {why_it_matters}. Read more: {url}"
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need scheduling logic (thread at publish, single tweet 2 days later, etc.)

# PLAN-342: Analytics feedback loop for content generation

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.20

## Hypothesis

We track challenge failures but not post-publish performance. Without knowing what actually resonates, we can't improve generation. Need closed-loop learning.

## Research Required

1. What metrics best predict content quality? (Engagement rate vs reach?)
2. How quickly can we learn from performance data?
3. How do we avoid overfitting to one viral post?
4. What's the minimum data needed for meaningful patterns?
5. How do algorithms affect what we can learn?

## Metrics to Track

### X Posts
| Metric | Weight | Why |
|--------|--------|-----|
| Retweet rate | High | 20x algorithm value |
| Reply rate | High | 13.5x algorithm value |
| Bookmark rate | Medium | Quality signal |
| Profile clicks | Medium | Interest signal |
| Like rate | Low | Lowest algorithm value |

### Blog Posts
| Metric | Weight | Why |
|--------|--------|-----|
| Time on page | High | Engagement signal |
| Scroll depth | High | Content quality |
| Click-through to product | High | Conversion |
| Social shares | Medium | Virality |
| Bounce rate | Medium (inverse) | Hook quality |

## Proposed Solution

```python
@dataclass
class ContentPerformance:
    content_id: str
    content_type: str  # "x_post" | "blog_post"
    metrics: dict
    features: dict  # What made this post unique

def analyze_performance_patterns():
    """
    Find patterns in high-performing content.
    """
    high_performers = get_top_percentile(posts, 20)
    low_performers = get_bottom_percentile(posts, 20)

    patterns = {
        "hooks_that_work": extract_hook_patterns(high_performers),
        "hooks_that_fail": extract_hook_patterns(low_performers),
        "optimal_length": analyze_length_correlation(all_posts),
        "best_topics": analyze_topic_performance(all_posts),
        "best_times": analyze_timing_correlation(all_posts),
    }

    return patterns

def update_generation_prompt(patterns: dict):
    """
    Feed learnings back into content generation.
    """
    prompt_additions = []

    if patterns["hooks_that_work"]:
        prompt_additions.append(
            f"Hooks that performed well: {patterns['hooks_that_work'][:3]}"
        )

    if patterns["hooks_that_fail"]:
        prompt_additions.append(
            f"Avoid hooks like: {patterns['hooks_that_fail'][:3]}"
        )

    return prompt_additions
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need Typefully API for X metrics
- Need analytics (Plausible/GA) for blog metrics
- Need storage for historical performance data
- Need periodic analysis job (weekly?)
- Risk: Small sample size leads to wrong conclusions
- Mitigation: Require minimum 20 posts before drawing conclusions

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Track metrics for 100% of published content
- [ ] Weekly analysis report generated
- [ ] Generation prompt updated monthly with learnings
- [ ] Measurable improvement in engagement over time

# PLAN-343: Draft queue and graveyard management

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.15

## Hypothesis

Posts that fail challenge 3x get escalated to human... then disappear. Good ideas die in the graveyard. Need a system to track, review, and potentially resurrect failed drafts.

## Research Required

1. What % of failed drafts have salvageable value?
2. How often should the graveyard be reviewed?
3. Should failed drafts inform future generation (negative examples)?
4. What's the shelf life of a draft before it's stale?

## Proposed Solution

### Draft Lifecycle

```
┌─────────────┐
│  Generated  │
└──────┬──────┘
       │ Challenge
       ▼
┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│   Passed    │────▶│  Published  │
└─────────────┘     └─────────────┘
       │
       │ Failed
       ▼
┌─────────────┐
│   Rewrite   │ (up to 3x)
└──────┬──────┘
       │ Still failed
       ▼
┌─────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐
│  Graveyard  │────▶│  Reviewed   │
└─────────────┘     └──────┬──────┘
                           │
              ┌────────────┼────────────┐
              ▼            ▼            ▼
        ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐ ┌──────────┐
        │ Salvage  │ │  Delete  │ │  Defer   │
        │ (rewrite)│ │          │ │ (later)  │
        └──────────┘ └──────────┘ └──────────┘
```

### Draft Storage

```python
@dataclass
class Draft:
    id: str
    plan_id: str
    content_type: str
    content: str
    created_at: datetime
    challenge_attempts: int
    failure_reasons: list[str]
    status: str  # "pending", "published", "graveyard", "deleted"

def save_draft(draft: Draft):
    """Persist draft for later review."""
    with open(DRAFTS_FILE, "a") as f:
        f.write(json.dumps(asdict(draft)) + "\n")

def get_graveyard_drafts() -> list[Draft]:
    """Get drafts that failed and need review."""
    return [d for d in load_drafts() if d.status == "graveyard"]
```

### Weekly Review Process

```bash
# CLI command
crux bip review-graveyard

# Shows:
# - 5 drafts in graveyard
# - For each: original content, failure reasons, plan context
# - Options: [s]alvage, [d]elete, [l]ater
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need persistent draft storage (JSONL file)
- Need CLI for graveyard review
- Need to track failure reasons for pattern analysis
- Risk: Graveyard grows unbounded
- Mitigation: Auto-delete drafts older than 30 days

## Success Criteria

# PLAN-344: Competitor and trend awareness for content timing

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.25

## Hypothesis

Posting in a vacuum misses opportunities. If everyone's talking about Claude Code updates, our crux posts should ride that wave. Competitor awareness helps with timing and positioning.

## Research Required

1. Who are our competitors and adjacent players?
2. What signals indicate a trending topic?
3. How fast can we react to trends?
4. Is trend-jacking authentic or opportunistic?
5. What's the risk of appearing reactive vs proactive?

## Competitors/Adjacent to Monitor

### Direct Competitors
- Cursor (rules, memory)
- Continue.dev
- Aider
- Other AI coding context tools

### Ecosystem Players
- Claude Code (Anthropic)
- GitHub Copilot
- OpenAI/ChatGPT coding
- Windsurf

### Communities
- Indie Hackers
- Build in Public hashtag
- AI coding tool discussions

## Proposed Solution

### Trend Detection

```python
def detect_relevant_trends() -> list[Trend]:
    """
    Monitor for trending topics we should engage with.
    """
    trends = []

    # Twitter/X trending
    x_trends = fetch_x_trends(topics=["AI", "coding", "developer tools"])

    # Hacker News front page
    hn_posts = fetch_hn_frontpage()
    relevant_hn = [p for p in hn_posts if is_relevant(p, KEYWORDS)]

    # Competitor activity
    competitor_posts = fetch_competitor_tweets(COMPETITORS)
    high_engagement = [p for p in competitor_posts if p.engagement > threshold]

    return trends

KEYWORDS = [
    "AI coding", "Claude", "Cursor", "context", "memory",
    "coding assistant", "developer tools", "IDE", "MCP",
]
```

### Trend Response Options

def should_post_now(draft: Draft) -> tuple[bool, str]:
    """
    Check if now is good timing for this content.
    """
    trends = detect_relevant_trends()

    # Boost: Related trend is hot
    if any(trend_matches(t, draft) for t in trends):

        return False, "Weekend - schedule for Tuesday"

    return True, "No timing concerns"
```

# PLAN-346: Thread vs single tweet decision logic

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.15

## Hypothesis

Not everything needs to be a 3-tweet thread. Sometimes a single punchy tweet outperforms. The system should intelligently decide format based on content.

## Research Required

1. When do threads outperform single tweets?
2. What's the engagement difference by content type?
3. Is there thread fatigue? (Too many threads = less engagement?)
4. What's the optimal mix of threads vs singles?
5. Do followers have format preferences?

## Format Decision Matrix

| Content Type | Best Format | Why |
|--------------|-------------|-----|
| Quick win/milestone | Single tweet | Punchy, shareable |
| Feature launch | Thread (3-5) | Needs explanation |
| Tutorial/how-to | Thread (7-12) | Step-by-step |
| Hot take | Single tweet | Stands alone |
| Behind-the-scenes | Single + image | Visual focus |
| Story/journey | Thread (5-8) | Narrative arc |
| Announcement | Single + thread reply | Hook + details |

## Proposed Solution

```python
def decide_format(content: Content) -> str:
    """
    Decide optimal format for content.

    Returns: "single" | "thread" | "single_with_reply_thread"
    """
    # Character count
    if len(content.text) <= 200:
        return "single"  # Fits comfortably in one tweet

    # Content type signals
    if content.has_steps or content.has_numbered_list:
        return "thread"

    if content.is_milestone or content.is_metric:
        return "single"  # Numbers are punchy

    if content.has_link and content.needs_context:
        return "single_with_reply_thread"  # Hook + details in reply

    # Default based on substance
    if content.word_count > 100:
        return "thread"

    return "single"

def generate_content(plan: Plan) -> Content:
    """Generate content, then decide format."""
    content = generate_raw_content(plan)
    format_type = decide_format(content)

    if format_type == "single":
        return condense_to_single(content)
    elif format_type == "thread":
        return expand_to_thread(content)
    else:
        return create_hook_with_reply(content)
```

### Thread Length Optimization

```python
def optimal_thread_length(content_type: str) -> int:
    """Research-backed optimal lengths."""
    return {
        "tutorial": 8,      # Step-by-step needs space
        "story": 6,         # Narrative arc
        "feature": 4,       # Hook + problem + solution + CTA
        "announcement": 3,  # Minimal context needed
    }.get(content_type, 5)
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need condensation logic (thread → single)
- Need expansion logic (single → thread)
- Risk: Wrong format choice hurts engagement
- Mitigation: Track format performance, adjust heuristics

# PLAN-347: Accessibility in content generation

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.15

## Hypothesis

Accessible content reaches more people and demonstrates care. Alt text for images, readable formatting, and not relying on visual-only elements should be standard.

## Research Required

1. What % of developer audience uses assistive technology?
2. What are X/Twitter's accessibility features?
3. How do screen readers handle threads?
4. What's the readability level we should target?
5. Do accessible posts perform differently?

## Accessibility Requirements

### Images
- [ ] Alt text for all images
- [ ] Alt text describes content, not just "screenshot"
- [ ] Avoid text-only images (use real text)

### Text
- [ ] Avoid emoji-only communication
- [ ] CamelCase hashtags (#BuildInPublic not #buildinpublic)
- [ ] Readable contrast in any graphics
- [ ] Plain language (8th grade reading level)

### Structure
- [ ] Logical reading order in threads
- [ ] Numbered tweets for thread navigation
- [ ] Avoid ASCII art or text decorations

### Links
- [ ] Descriptive link text (not "click here")
- [ ] Full URLs visible (not just hyperlinked text)

## Proposed Solution

```python
def ensure_accessibility(content: Content) -> Content:
    """
    Apply accessibility improvements to content.
    """
    # Fix hashtags
    content.text = camel_case_hashtags(content.text)

    # Check reading level
    reading_level = calculate_flesch_kincaid(content.text)
    if reading_level > 8:
        content.warnings.append(f"Reading level {reading_level} - consider simplifying")

    # Ensure alt text
    for image in content.images:
        if not image.alt_text:
            image.alt_text = generate_alt_text(image)

    # Thread numbering
    if content.is_thread:
        content = add_thread_numbers(content)

    return content

def generate_alt_text(image: Image) -> str:
    """
    Generate descriptive alt text for image.
    """
    if image.type == "terminal_screenshot":
        return f"Terminal showing: {image.command} with output: {summarize(image.output)}"
    elif image.type == "code_snippet":
        return f"Code snippet in {image.language}: {describe_code(image.code)}"
    elif image.type == "diagram":
        return f"Diagram showing {image.title}: {image.description}"
    else:
        return image.description or "Image"

def camel_case_hashtags(text: str) -> str:
    """Convert hashtags to CamelCase for screen readers."""
    # #buildinpublic → #BuildInPublic
    import re
    def camel_case(match):
        tag = match.group(1)
        # Simple word boundary detection
        words = re.findall(r'[a-z]+', tag.lower())
        return '#' + ''.join(w.capitalize() for w in words)

    return re.sub(r'#([a-zA-Z]+)', camel_case, text)
```

## Implementation Implications

- Need alt text generation (can be AI-assisted)
- Need reading level calculation (textstat library)
- Need hashtag formatting
- Risk: Over-engineering simple content
- Mitigation: Only apply where needed, don't block on minor issues

## Success Criteria

- [ ] 100% of images have alt text
- [ ] All hashtags use CamelCase
- [ ] Average reading level ≤ 8th grade
- [ ] No accessibility complaints from users

# PLAN-348: Posting cadence management

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.20

## Hypothesis

Dumping 5 posts in one day then nothing for a week hurts growth. Consistent cadence builds audience habits and algorithm favor. Need intelligent spacing.

## Research Required

1. What's the optimal posting frequency for growth?
2. How much spacing between posts?
3. Does posting too much hurt engagement per post?
4. What's the algorithm's view on posting frequency?
5. How do successful accounts pace themselves?

## Research Findings (from PLAN-337)

- 3-7 tweets/day for 1k-10k follower accounts
- 5-10 tweets/day for 10k+ accounts
- 70% engaging, 30% creating
- Best times: Tue-Thu 9AM-2PM EST
- Avoid: 3-5PM weekdays, weekends

## Proposed Solution

### Cadence Rules

```python
CADENCE_CONFIG = {
    "min_hours_between_posts": 2,
    "max_posts_per_day": 5,
    "optimal_posts_per_day": 3,
    "quiet_days": ["Saturday", "Sunday"],
    "peak_hours": [(9, 11), (13, 15)],  # EST
}

def schedule_post(draft: Draft) -> datetime:
    """
    Find optimal slot for this post.
    """
    now = datetime.now(EST)
    recent_posts = get_posts_last_24h()

    # Check daily limit
    if len(recent_posts) >= CADENCE_CONFIG["max_posts_per_day"]:
        return next_available_slot(tomorrow())

    # Check spacing
    if recent_posts:
        last_post_time = recent_posts[-1].posted_at
        min_next = last_post_time + timedelta(hours=2)
        if now < min_next:
            return min_next

    # Find peak hour slot
    for start, end in CADENCE_CONFIG["peak_hours"]:
        slot = now.replace(hour=start, minute=0)
        if slot > now and is_slot_available(slot):
            return slot

    # Default: next available
    return now
```

### Queue Management

```python
class PostQueue:
    def __init__(self):
        self.pending: list[Draft] = []
        self.scheduled: list[tuple[Draft, datetime]] = []

    def add(self, draft: Draft, priority: int = 0):
        """Add draft to queue with priority."""
        self.pending.append((draft, priority))
        self.pending.sort(key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
        self.rebalance_schedule()

    def rebalance_schedule(self):
        """
        Redistribute scheduled posts for optimal cadence.
        """
        all_drafts = self.pending + [d for d, _ in self.scheduled]
        self.scheduled = []

        for draft in all_drafts:
            slot = schedule_post(draft)
            self.scheduled.append((draft, slot))
```

### Priority System

| Content Type | Priority | Why |
|--------------|----------|-----|
| Breaking/timely | 10 | Must post now |
| Trend response | 8 | Time-sensitive |
| Feature launch | 6 | Important but flexible |
| Regular content | 4 | Can wait |
| Evergreen | 2 | No rush |

## Implementation Implications

- Need post scheduling system (queue)
- Need Typefully integration for scheduled posts
- Need priority system
- Risk: Queue backup during low-posting periods
- Mitigation: Auto-schedule backlog, don't let queue grow >10

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Never >5 posts in one day
- [ ] Never <1 post on weekdays
- [ ] Minimum 2 hours between posts
- [ ] 80%+ of posts in peak hours
- [ ] Consistent daily presence

# PLAN-349: Evergreen content detection and resharing

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.15

## Hypothesis

Some content is timely (launches), some is evergreen (tutorials, insights). Evergreen content should be periodically reshared to reach new followers. Currently we post once and forget.

## Research Required

1. What makes content evergreen vs timely?
2. How often can you reshare without annoying followers?
3. Should reshares be identical or reframed?
4. What's the decay curve of post reach?
5. Do reshares perform as well as originals?

## Evergreen Criteria

### IS Evergreen
- Tutorials and how-tos
- Conceptual explanations
- Best practices
- Tool comparisons
- Lessons learned
- Philosophy/approach posts

### NOT Evergreen
- Launch announcements
- Milestone updates (specific dates)
- News commentary
- Bug fix announcements
- Version-specific content

## Proposed Solution

```python
def classify_content(post: Post) -> str:
    """Classify as evergreen or timely."""
    timely_signals = [
        "just shipped",
        "today",
        "this week",
        "v1.2.3",  # version numbers
        "announcing",
        re.match(r'\d{4}-\d{2}-\d{2}', post.text),  # dates
    ]

    if any(signal in post.text.lower() for signal in timely_signals):
        return "timely"

    evergreen_signals = [
        "how to",
        "why",
        "the secret",
        "lesson learned",
        "pattern",
        "best practice",
    ]

    if any(signal in post.text.lower() for signal in evergreen_signals):
        return "evergreen"

    return "unknown"

def schedule_reshares(posts: list[Post]):
    """
    Schedule reshares of evergreen content.
    """
    evergreen = [p for p in posts if p.content_type == "evergreen"]

    for post in evergreen:
        # Only reshare if performed well
        if post.engagement_rate < 0.02:
            continue

        # Schedule reshare 30-60 days later
        reshare_date = post.posted_at + timedelta(days=random.randint(30, 60))

        # Reframe slightly
        reshared = reframe_for_reshare(post)
        schedule_post(reshared, reshare_date)

def reframe_for_reshare(post: Post) -> Post:
    """
    Slightly modify post for reshare.
    """
    reframe_options = [
        f"Reminder: {post.text}",
        f"Still true: {post.text}",
        f"This keeps coming up: {post.text}",
        post.text,  # Sometimes just repost as-is
    ]

    new_text = random.choice(reframe_options)
    return Post(text=new_text, original_id=post.id)
```

### Reshare Rules

| Metric | Threshold |
|--------|-----------|
| Min days since original | 30 |
| Min engagement rate | 2% |
| Max reshares per post | 3 |
| Max reshares per week | 2 |

## Implementation Implications

- Need content classification
- Need engagement tracking
- Need reshare scheduling
- Risk: Feeling repetitive
- Mitigation: Reframe, space out, limit reshares

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Evergreen content identified at publish time
- [ ] Top 20% evergreen posts reshared
- [ ] Reshares scheduled 30-60 days out
- [ ] No complaints about repetitive content
- [ ] Reshares reach new followers

# PLAN-350: A/B testing for content optimization

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.25

## Hypothesis

Without testing, we're guessing what works. A/B testing different hooks, formats, and CTAs would provide data to improve generation. X's fast feedback loop makes this feasible.

## Research Required

1. How to A/B test on X without annoying followers?
2. What sample size needed for significance?
3. What variables are worth testing?
4. How to avoid algorithm effects confounding results?
5. What tools support X A/B testing?

## Testing Framework

### What to Test

| Variable | Test Method | Metric |
|----------|-------------|--------|
| Hook style | Different first tweet | Reply rate |
| Thread length | 3 vs 7 tweets | Completion rate |
| CTA wording | Different final tweet | Click rate |
| Timing | Same content, different times | Impressions |
| Visual vs text | Image vs no image | Engagement |
| Question vs statement | Hook format | Replies |

### Testing Methods

**Method 1: Sequential Testing**
Post version A on Tuesday, version B on Thursday. Compare metrics.
- Pro: Simple
- Con: Time/day effects confound results

**Method 2: Audience Splitting**
Post both versions, delete lower performer after 30 min.
- Pro: Same conditions
- Con: Algorithm may penalize deletion

**Method 3: Slow Rollout**
Post to subset first (close friends list), expand winner.
- Pro: Low risk
- Con: Limited initial reach

## Proposed Solution

```python
@dataclass
class ABTest:
    id: str
    variable: str
    variant_a: str
    variant_b: str
    metric: str
    status: str  # "running" | "completed"
    winner: str | None

def run_ab_test(test: ABTest):
    """
    Execute A/B test using sequential method.
    """
    # Post variant A
    post_a = generate_variant(test.variant_a)
    result_a = post_and_wait(post_a, hours=24)

    # Post variant B (2 days later, same time)
    post_b = generate_variant(test.variant_b)
    result_b = post_and_wait(post_b, hours=24)

    # Compare
    test.winner = "A" if result_a[test.metric] > result_b[test.metric] else "B"
    test.status = "completed"

    # Log learning
    log_ab_result(test)

def generate_variant(variant_config: dict) -> Post:
    """Generate post with specific variant applied."""
    base_content = generate_content()

    if variant_config["variable"] == "hook":
        base_content.hook = variant_config["value"]
    elif variant_config["variable"] == "cta":
        base_content.cta = variant_config["value"]
    # ... etc

    return base_content
```

### Test Queue

PENDING_TESTS = [
    ABTest(
        variable="hook_style",

        variant_b="statement",
        metric="reply_rate",
    ),

## Implementation Implications

- Need metric tracking per post
- Need statistical significance calculation
- Need test queue management

- Mitigation: Run tests over longer periods, focus on high-impact variables
- Risk: Algorithm changes confound results

# PLAN-351: Crisis circuit breaker for negative engagement

**Status:** planned
**Group:** GROUP-MKT
**Domain:** crux
**Risk:** 0.30

## Hypothesis

If a post is getting "ratio'd" (more negative replies than likes) or going viral for wrong reasons, we need to detect and respond quickly. Automated detection prevents damage while we sleep.

## Research Required

1. What signals indicate a post is going badly?
2. How fast can negative sentiment spread?
3. What's the right response? (Delete? Apologize? Clarify?)
4. When is controversy good vs bad?
5. What's the threshold for intervention?

## Crisis Signals

### Red Flags (Investigate)
- Reply-to-like ratio > 2:1
- Quote tweets > retweets (often criticism)
- Rapid follower loss
- Mentions from accounts with "drama" keywords in bio

### Critical (Immediate Action)
- Community Notes added
- Reply-to-like ratio > 5:1
- Major account criticism
- Factual errors pointed out
- Offensive interpretation spreading

## Proposed Solution

```python
def monitor_post_health(post_id: str):
    """
    Continuously monitor post for crisis signals.
    """
    while post_age(post_id) < timedelta(hours=24):
        metrics = fetch_post_metrics(post_id)
        sentiment = analyze_reply_sentiment(post_id)

        health_score = calculate_health_score(metrics, sentiment)

        if health_score < CRITICAL_THRESHOLD:
            trigger_crisis_response(post_id, "critical")
            return

        if health_score < WARNING_THRESHOLD:
            notify_for_review(post_id, "warning")

        sleep(300)  # Check every 5 minutes

def calculate_health_score(metrics: dict, sentiment: dict) -> float:
    """
    Score from 0 (crisis) to 1 (healthy).
    """
    score = 1.0

    # Reply ratio
    if metrics["replies"] > metrics["likes"] * 2:
        score -= 0.3

    # Quote tweet ratio
    if metrics["quote_tweets"] > metrics["retweets"]:
        score -= 0.2

    # Negative sentiment
    if sentiment["negative_pct"] > 0.3:
        score -= 0.3

    # Community notes
    if metrics["has_community_note"]:
        score -= 0.5

    return max(0, score)

def trigger_crisis_response(post_id: str, severity: str):
    """
    Respond to crisis.
    """
    if severity == "critical":
        # Immediate notification
        send_urgent_alert(
            f"CRITICAL: Post {post_id} in crisis. "
            f"Review immediately. Consider deletion."
        )

        # Auto-actions (optional)
        # hide_post(post_id)  # If enabled

    elif severity == "warning":
        send_notification(
            f"WARNING: Post {post_id} showing negative signals. "
            f"Monitor closely."
        )
```

### Response Playbook

| Situation | Response |
|-----------|----------|
| Factual error | Correct publicly, thank corrector |
| Misinterpretation | Clarify in reply thread |
| Offensive (unintended) | Apologize, delete if severe |
| Controversial (intended) | Monitor, don't engage trolls |
| Pile-on from competitors | Document, don't engage |
| Community Note | Verify accuracy, respond if wrong |

## Implementation Implications

- Need X API for reply/quote monitoring
- Need sentiment analysis (can be simple keyword-based)
- Need alerting system (SMS, Slack, etc.)
- Need response playbook documentation
- Risk: False positives causing panic
- Mitigation: Warning tier before critical, human review
- Risk: Over-reacting to normal debate
- Mitigation: Thresholds tuned over time

## Success Criteria

- [ ] All posts monitored for first 24 hours
- [ ] Warning alerts within 30 minutes of signals
- [ ] Critical alerts within 5 minutes
- [ ] No undetected crises
- [ ] Response time <1 hour for critical
- [ ] Clear playbook for each scenario
